title: Open-issues backlog clearance: proactive sweep + EXP-tied auto-close + carry-over enforcement, with burn-down vs BAU mode controls
scope_authority: Design proposal only (PROP-NNN). Operator reviews before implementation. Two backfills (the immediate burn-down and the structural fix) are both in scope for the proposal — operator may approve one, the other, or both.
context: open-issues.json currently holds 231 status='open' issues plus 24 with terminal-ish statuses (closed/fixed/wontfix/monitoring/blocked/assigned/etc.) that should already have been archived to closed-issues.json — total file size 255 entries.
Age distribution of the 231 status='open' items: ≤1d=12, ≤3d=5, ≤7d=16, ≤14d=120, ≤21d=71, ≤30d=7, >30d=0. The 30-day OBE threshold is days away — ISS-708 (29d) crosses tomorrow if no action.
Source breakdown: 168 of 231 (73%) come from curmudgeon's review cycles (WIN-XXX.cN finds). 88 of 231 (38%) explicitly mention an EXP-NNN by ID in their description.
Severity breakdown of status='open': minor=160, moderate=64, major=6, info=1. The bulk is housekeeping minors that aren't blocking anything but accumulate faster than they get cleared.

Diagnosis: the decider's dispatcher (decider.md Priority 1–5) has no slot for proactive open-issues.json scan-and-clear. Issues only close through three reactive paths: (a) curmudgeon's next review of the affected WIN flags them as fixed AND that round's batchability gate happens to include them, (b) an EXP integration explicitly closes them via `closes: [...]`, (c) a self-apply patch in the run mentions the ISS by ID. Anything that doesn't get patched in the round it was created has no built-in path back to the front of the queue.
Concrete failure modes documented in the current state:
• ISS-1547 (14d, info): 'EXP-249 ready — pending curmudgeon review' — but EXP-249 was integrated long ago. No EXP-tied auto-close exists, so the ISS sits.

• ISS-1244/1245/1246 (19d, minor): all carry 'still open from c4' annotations in their descriptions. Curmudgeon found them in c4, decider didn't patch, curmudgeon's c5 re-flagged them — and decider STILL didn't act. The 'still open from c-N' annotation is the curmudgeon's polite way of saying 'we already told you about this' but there's no formalized re-attempt rule.
• ISS-708 (29d, minor): self-tagged 'Lower priority than content work' by decider when created. Genuinely deferrable but at the OBE threshold.

Operator framing: decider has plenty of throughput per run; the issue isn't capacity. The dispatcher routes work TO decider, but stale items aren't routed. They sit in a parking lot the dispatcher doesn't visit. The fix is structural, not a per-run cap change.
operator_intent: Two interlocking goals. (1) Burn down the existing 231-item backlog SAFELY (no wrong-closes that hide live problems) and QUICKLY (clear before issues cross the 30-day OBE threshold over the next 1-2 weeks). (2) Prevent the same accumulation from recurring by adding structural mechanisms to the decider's normal flow. The proposal should treat these as a coordinated package, not separate efforts — burn-down is the one-shot cleanup using the same mechanisms that the BAU fix installs permanently.

tasks.description: Design and propose three complementary mechanisms (operator picks which to implement; they are not mutually exclusive). Evaluate each on safety/value/implementation cost.

mechanisms_to_evaluate.summary: Decider Priority 5b: scan open-issues.json for issues > N days old and either patch (if scope-appropriate), wontfix-with-rationale (if no longer relevant per current state of the codebase), or escalate to operator-required.
mechanisms_to_evaluate.purpose: Catches anything that fell through M2 and M3. The durable fix.
key_design_questions: What N (age threshold) triggers the sweep? Options: 14d, 21d, 28d.
How often does the sweep run? Every decider run? Once per N runs? Once per day?
What's the per-sweep cap (max items processed per run)? 5? 10? 20?
Does the sweep act differently by severity (auto-wontfix minors, manual-escalate moderates+majors)?
Where does the sweep get its 'is this still real' signal — re-read the source citation, re-grep the codebase, ask analyst, etc.?

mechanisms_to_evaluate.summary: When decider integrates an EXP-NNN, auto-search open-issues.json for any ISS whose description/title/source/related_expansion field references EXP-NNN. Auto-close those ISSs with reason 'EXP-NNN integrated by run XXX, ISS no longer relevant'.
mechanisms_to_evaluate.purpose: Closes the most common hygiene gap. Catches ISS-1547-like cases. Lowest-risk highest-immediate-value piece.
key_design_questions: What constitutes a match? Exact 'EXP-NNN' substring, or also `assigned_exp` field, or also `expansion_file` path?
Does auto-close apply only to ISSs in 'pending curmudgeon review' / 'pending integration' status, or to any open ISS mentioning the EXP?

What if the ISS describes a finding that the EXP didn't actually fix (false-positive close)? Need a guard — maybe 'ISS title says ready/pending → safe to close; ISS title says found unfixed bug → require manual review'.
Does this run on every EXP integration, or only on EXPs flagged with `closes_iss: [...]`?

mechanisms_to_evaluate.summary: When curmudgeon writes a c-(N+1) review with 'still open from c-N' annotations on issues, decider treats those annotations as MUST-process in the same run — patch or wontfix-with-rationale, don't just create a new ISS or leave the old one untouched. Formalize the half-implemented pattern.
mechanisms_to_evaluate.purpose: Prevents the c4→c5→c6 ratchet where minor findings flag forever without action. Surgical fix to the most visible bottleneck (WIN-053 cluster).
key_design_questions: How does curmudgeon signal 'still open from c-N' machine-readably? Free text in description (current pattern, brittle) or new field on the review JSON like `unresolved_prior_cycle: [ISS-NNN, ...]`?
What action is decider required to take on each carry-over ISS? Patch attempt? Wontfix decision? Operator escalation?
What if decider deems the carry-over ISS is no longer relevant (e.g., the section was rewritten and the issue moot)? Need a 'wontfix with carryover-rationale' path.
Does this interact with the curmudgeon batch-gate (DIRECTIVE-20260509-001 / PROP-025)? If a c5 verification batch contains carry-overs from c4, are those carry-over fixes part of the batchable scope?

tasks.description: Design the operator-facing controls that let decider operate in two distinct modes against open-issues.json: BAU (steady-state, prevents accumulation) and BURN-DOWN (one-shot or time-bounded acceleration to clear an existing backlog SAFELY and QUICKLY). The same M1/M2/M3 mechanisms underlie both modes — what changes is the rate, scope, safety controls, and termination criteria.
key_design_questions: How does the operator engage burn-down mode? Options: (a) toggle field in priority-queue.json or status.json (e.g., `decider_mode: 'burndown'`); (b) HNOTE with specific subject prefix; (c) tinker DIRECTIVE; (d) flag file in monitor/decisions/.

How does burn-down mode terminate? Options: (a) auto-revert when open-issues count drops below threshold T (e.g., T=50); (b) auto-revert after X consecutive runs with zero closures (no more low-hanging fruit); (c) auto-revert after Y elapsed days; (d) operator manual revert; (e) combination.

What safety controls prevent wrong-closes during burn-down? Options: (a) dry-run mode that lists what WOULD be closed without closing, operator approves batch; (b) per-run cap rises in burn-down (e.g., 50 vs 10 in BAU); (c) severity tier — auto-close minors freely, hold moderates for manual review, never auto-close majors; (d) 'recently-touched' guard — never auto-close an ISS modified in the last 48 hours; (e) every burn-down closure generates an entry in a closure-ledger.jsonl that's revertable.

What rate is 'safely fast'? Estimate: 10 closures/run × 6 runs/day × M1+M2+M3 hit rate. If hit rate is 60% and we want to clear 200 items, that's ~33 effective runs ≈ 5-6 days. Is that fast enough? Too fast?

Should burn-down mode interact with curmudgeon? E.g., suspend new curmudgeon-driven ISS creation for the burn-down period to prevent 'one-step-forward-one-step-back' churn? Or let curmudgeon keep finding things while decider burns down older items?

What's the rollback procedure if a burn-down closure was wrong? The closure-ledger should make this easy — single command to re-open every ISS closed by run X. Define the format and the rollback script.

tasks.description: Given the OBE pressure (ISS-708 crosses 30d tomorrow, ~78 items will cross within 2 weeks), recommend an implementation order that minimizes time-to-first-clearance while keeping safety controls in place. Strawman ordering for the proposal to evaluate:

ordering_options.summary: Implement M2 (EXP-tied auto-close) immediately as a one-shot script that operator runs once. Closes ~88 EXP-mentioning items in minutes. Then implement M1 and M3 as the durable BAU fix. Burn-down mode never engages because the immediate spike is handled by the one-shot.
ordering_options.pro: Fastest time-to-relief. M2 is the lowest-risk mechanism. One-shot script is easy to dry-run and review.
ordering_options.con: Still leaves ~140 non-EXP-mentioning items aging. M1/M3 land later but need to clear the residue.

ordering_options.summary: Implement M1+M2+M3 as one coherent change. Add burn-down toggle. Operator engages burn-down for first 5-7 days post-deployment to clear the backlog. Toggle reverts to BAU when open-issues count drops below 50.
ordering_options.pro: Single integrated change. Burn-down/BAU distinction is clean. Operator has explicit control.
ordering_options.con: Larger blast radius — landing all three mechanisms simultaneously is riskier. Single-shot review burden.

ordering_options.summary: Phase 1: M2 + burn-down toggle, deployed together. Burn-down clears EXP-mentioning items within 1-2 days. Phase 2 (1 week later): M1 + M3 land in BAU, operator decides whether to re-engage burn-down for the residue.
ordering_options.pro: Phased risk. Gets immediate relief from M2 while M1/M3 design has time to settle.
ordering_options.con: Two coordination points, two reviews, two integrations.

operator_questions: Q1 (mechanism scope): Implement all three (M1+M2+M3), or pick a subset? If subset, which?

Q2 (burn-down trigger and revert): What's the engagement mechanism (HNOTE, flag file, status field) and what's the revert trigger (count threshold, run-count, manual)? Tinker should propose 1-2 specific options.

Q3 (safety controls during burn-down): Required minimum: (a) closure-ledger that's revertable; (b) severity tier — auto-close minors only, manual-review for moderates+majors; (c) dry-run mode at first. Are these sufficient, or are additional controls needed?
Q4 (per-run cap): Tinker should propose specific BAU cap and burn-down cap values. Strawman: BAU=10/run, burn-down=30/run. Justify based on context budget and safety.
Q5 (interaction with curmudgeon): During burn-down, does curmudgeon continue running (and creating new ISSs that age into the same problem), or pause/throttle? Tinker proposes interaction rule.

Q6 (M2 false-positive guard): Concrete heuristic for distinguishing 'EXP integrated, ISS resolved' (safe to auto-close) from 'EXP integrated, ISS describes a separate finding the EXP didn't fix' (must NOT auto-close). Tinker proposes specific match rules with examples.
Q7 (M3 schema): New machine-readable field for curmudgeon's carry-over annotations? Free-text grep with explicit pattern? Tinker recommends.
Q8 (implementation order): Tinker recommends among order_A / order_B / order_C, with rationale.

Q9 (24 stale-statused entries): What's the right disposition for the 24 entries currently in open-issues.json with terminal-ish statuses (fixed/closed/wontfix/monitoring/blocked/assigned/etc.)? One-shot archiver to closed-issues.json, or wait for the next decider run that touches each? Tinker proposes.
completion_criteria: Tinker authors PROP-NNN covering all three mechanisms (M1, M2, M3) and burn-down vs BAU mode controls.
PROP includes recommendations for all 9 operator questions, with rationale for each.
PROP includes concrete implementation paths for whichever ordering tinker recommends.
PROP includes the rollback procedure for any auto-close (closure-ledger format, revert script).
PROP includes a worked example for M2 false-positive guard (Q6) showing how 3-5 of the current 88 EXP-mentioning open issues would be classified.
DIRECTIVE-20260510-001 marked status=completed, completed_at, completed_by_run, prop_id_authored fields filled per DIRECTIVE-LIFECYCLE rule.
cross_references: monitor/prompts/decider.md — Priority 1–5 dispatcher (where M1 plugs in)
monitor/prompts/reference/decider-curmudgeon.md — EXP integration logic (where M2 plugs in)
monitor/prompts/reference/decider-patches-and-selfapply.md — self-apply patch flow (where carry-over might be enforced)
monitor/prompts/curmudgeon.md / curmudgeon-change-and-holistic.md — where M3 carry-over annotation schema would be added
DIRECTIVE-20260509-001 (PROP-025) — curmudgeon batch-gate content-based; relevant because M3 interacts with batchability classification
PROP-024 — workspace-sync JSON validity gate (separate, but tinker noted it as 12-day-pending; same backlog-clearance theme)
